---
title: "When Games Hit the Big Screen"
subtitle: "Box office, profitability, and ratings of video-game films"
author: "Pascal Muellner<br>[https://youtu.be/pmzR2FUBvkQ](https://youtu.be/pmzR2FUBvkQ)"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: ['data/customtheming.scss']
    slide-number: true
    margin: 0.02
    width: 1100
    background-image: "images/watercolour_sys02_img34_teacup-ocean.jpg"
resources:
  - images/
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
---
```{r}
#| label: setup-questions
#| include: false
pacman::p_load(tidyverse)
theme_set(theme_minimal(base_size = 13))

game_films <- read_csv("data/game_films.csv")
video_game_ratings <- read_csv("data/video_game_ratings.csv")

# getting cpi data for inflation adjustment
cu <- read_tsv("data/cpi-data.csv", na = c("", "NA", "-")) |>   # "-" marks a withheld value
  mutate(series_id = str_trim(series_id)) |>
  filter(series_id == "CUUR0000SA0")

cpi_annual <- cu |>                          # BLS official annual average (period M13)
  filter(period == "M13") |>
  transmute(year, cpi = value)

cpi_current <- cu |>                         # current year (2026) has no annual yet:
  filter(period %in% c("M01", "M02", "M03", "M04", "M05", "M06",
                       "M07", "M08", "M09", "M10", "M11", "M12"),
         !year %in% cpi_annual$year) |>      # average its available monthly values
  group_by(year) |>
  summarise(cpi = mean(value, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")

cpi <- bind_rows(cpi_annual, cpi_current) |> arrange(year)
cpi_base <- cpi |> filter(year == 2025) |> pull(cpi)  # choosing 2025 for most recent complete year

# profitability and box office (Question 1)
q1_profitability <- game_films |>
  filter(
    worldwide_box_office_currency == "$",
    !is.na(worldwide_box_office),
    !is.na(budget_low),
    budget_currency == "$"
  ) |>
  mutate(
    year = year(release_date),                                            
    release_era = factor(str_c(floor(year / 10) * 10, "s")),              # group into decades
    publisher = str_trim(str_extract(original_game_publisher, "^[^|]+")), # first studio listed (before any "|")
    publisher = fct_lump_min(publisher, min = 3),                         # keep publishers with 3+ films, rest -> "Other"
    budget_midpoint = (budget_low + budget_high) / 2,                     # budget estimate from the low-high range
    profit = worldwide_box_office - budget_midpoint,                      
    roi = worldwide_box_office / budget_midpoint,                         
    broke_even = worldwide_box_office >= budget_midpoint                  # TRUE if the film broke even or made a profit
  ) |>
  left_join(cpi, by = "year") |>                                          
  mutate(box_office_real = worldwide_box_office * cpi_base / cpi)         # adjusted for inflation as of 2025

n_profit <- nrow(q1_profitability)

# game vs. film rating by critics (Question 2)
# map each title with its franchise
assign_franchise <- function(title) {
  t <- str_to_lower(title)
  case_when(
    str_detect(t, "resident evil")           ~ "Resident Evil",
    str_detect(t, "pokémon|pokemon")         ~ "Pokemon",
    str_detect(t, "mortal kombat")           ~ "Mortal Kombat",
    str_detect(t, "sonic")                   ~ "Sonic",
    str_detect(t, "street fighter")          ~ "Street Fighter",
    str_detect(t, "tomb raider|lara croft")  ~ "Tomb Raider",
    str_detect(t, "final fantasy")           ~ "Final Fantasy",
    str_detect(t, "silent hill")             ~ "Silent Hill",
    str_detect(t, "mario")                   ~ "Super Mario",
    str_detect(t, "hitman")                  ~ "Hitman",
    str_detect(t, "angry birds")             ~ "Angry Birds",
    str_detect(t, "assassin.?s creed")       ~ "Assassins Creed",
    str_detect(t, "\\bdoom\\b")              ~ "Doom",
    str_detect(t, "need for speed")          ~ "Need for Speed",
    str_detect(t, "prince of persia")        ~ "Prince of Persia",
    str_detect(t, "warcraft")                ~ "Warcraft",
    str_detect(t, "uncharted")               ~ "Uncharted",
    str_detect(t, "max payne")               ~ "Max Payne",
    str_detect(t, "alone in the dark")       ~ "Alone in the Dark",
    str_detect(t, "bloodrayne")              ~ "BloodRayne",
    str_detect(t, "double dragon")           ~ "Double Dragon",
    str_detect(t, "dead or alive")           ~ "Dead or Alive",
    str_detect(t, "house of the dead")       ~ "House of the Dead",
    str_detect(t, "ratchet")                 ~ "Ratchet & Clank",
    str_detect(t, "\\brampage\\b")           ~ "Rampage",
    str_detect(t, "monster hunter")          ~ "Monster Hunter",
    str_detect(t, "borderlands")             ~ "Borderlands",
    str_detect(t, "dungeon siege")           ~ "Dungeon Siege",
    .default = NA_character_
  )
}
# games -> one row per franchise, averaging the games' Metacritic critic scores
game_ratings <- video_game_ratings |>
  mutate(franchise = assign_franchise(Name)) |>
  filter(!is.na(franchise)) |>
  group_by(franchise) |>
  summarise(game_score = mean(Critic_Score, na.rm = TRUE), n_games = n())

# films -> one row per franchise, averaging the films' metacritic, and choosing
# when the franchise first reached the screen
film_ratings <- game_films |>
  mutate(franchise = assign_franchise(title), year = year(release_date)) |>
  filter(!is.na(franchise)) |>
  group_by(franchise) |>
  summarise(
    film_score = mean(metacritic, na.rm = TRUE),
    n_films    = n(),
    first_year = min(year, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) |>
  mutate(
    decade = str_c(floor(first_year / 10) * 10, "s"),
    # merging single point entry from the 1980s to keep things consistent with Question 1
    release_era = fct_recode(factor(decade),
                             "1990s and earlier" = "1980s",
                             "1990s and earlier" = "1990s")
  )

# only keep franchises present in both tables
# I decided to use an inner join rather than the left_join I had initially proposed
# since I wanted to make sure franchises without a game or film match are excluded
q2_franchise_ratings <- film_ratings |>
  inner_join(game_ratings, by = "franchise") |>
  filter(!is.nan(film_score)) |>
  mutate(rating_gap = game_score - film_score)

q2_long <- q2_franchise_ratings |>
  select(franchise, game_score, film_score, rating_gap) |>
  pivot_longer(c(game_score, film_score), names_to = "type", values_to = "score") |>
  mutate(type = if_else(type == "game_score", "Game", "Film"))
```

## Two datasets, two questions

::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
**The data**

- **439** film adaptations of video games (TidyTuesday / Wikipedia) — box office, budgets, critic scores
- **\~16,900** games with Metacritic scores (Kaggle) — to compare films against their source games
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
**The questions**

1.  **Commercial:** how does box office vary by the game's *publisher* and *era*, and how often do these films break even?
2.  **Critical:** how do the films' review scores compare to their *games'* scores, and does that gap change over time/era?
:::
:::::

::: notes
:::

# Question 1: Profitability and Box Office

## Most films profit — but not in the 2000s

::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
```{r}
#| label: q1-break-even
#| fig-alt: "Four scatter panels, one per release era (1990s, 2000s, 2010s, 2020s), plotting worldwide box office against estimated budget on a logarithmic scale, each with a dashed break-even line. Blue dots represent adaptations that were profitable, while red dots show a loss. The 2000s facet has many red dots below the line, while the 2010s and 2020s panels show most of their blue dots above the line."
#| fig-width: 5
#| fig-height: 4.6
q1_plot_data <- q1_profitability |>
  mutate(result = factor(if_else(broke_even, "Profit", "Loss"),
                         levels = c("Profit", "Loss")))

ggplot(q1_plot_data, aes(budget_midpoint, worldwide_box_office, color = result)) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray50") +  # break-even line
  geom_point(size = 2, alpha = 0.9) +
  facet_wrap(~ release_era) +
  # using log scales since budgets and grosses span several orders of magnitude ($1M to >$1B)
  scale_x_log10(labels = scales::label_dollar(scale_cut = scales::cut_short_scale())) +
  scale_y_log10(labels = scales::label_dollar(scale_cut = scales::cut_short_scale())) +
  # added Set1's blue and red manually in order to keep profit on the left side
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Profit" = "#377EB8", "Loss" = "#E41A1C"), name = NULL) + 
  labs(
    x = "Production budget (midpoint)", y = "Worldwide box office"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(
    panel.spacing = unit(1.4, "lines"),
    panel.border = element_rect(color = "gray72", fill = NA, linewidth = 0.5),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "gray91", linewidth = 0.3),
    strip.background = element_rect(fill = "gray91", color = "gray72", linewidth = 0.5),
    strip.text = element_text(face = "bold", margin = margin(4, 0, 4, 0)),
    legend.position = "top"
  )
```
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
```{r}
#| label: q1-publisher
#| fig-alt: "Horizontal boxplot of inflation-adjusted worldwide box office for 60 adaptations grouped by source-game publisher, ordered by median, with each publisher's film count labeled. A dashed red line marks the overall median of about 171 million dollars, labeled at the top. While Nintendo, Sega and Eidos sit above it, Konami and Sony fall below. Overall, Ubisoft has the widest spread."
#| fig-width: 5
#| fig-height: 4.6
overall_med <- median(q1_profitability$box_office_real)

# appends each publisher's film count to its label, ordered by median box office
q1_publisher <- q1_profitability |>
  add_count(publisher, name = "pub_n") |>
  mutate(publisher_lab = fct_reorder(str_c(publisher, "  (", pub_n, " films)"),
                                     box_office_real, .fun = median))

ggplot(q1_publisher, aes(box_office_real, publisher_lab)) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = overall_med, linetype = "dashed", color = "#E41A1C", linewidth = 0.7) +  # overall median
  geom_boxplot(fill = "gray84", color = "gray42", outlier.color = "gray54", width = 0.6) +
  annotate("label", x = overall_med, y = 9.2,
           label = str_c("Overall median ($", round(overall_med / 1e6), "M)"),   # tracks the base year
           size = 3, color = "#E41A1C", fill = "white", label.size = 0.4) +
  scale_x_log10(labels = scales::label_dollar(scale_cut = scales::cut_short_scale())) +
  scale_y_discrete(expand = expansion(add = c(0.6, 1.3))) +
  coord_cartesian(clip = "off") +
  labs(
    x = "Worldwide box office\n (inflation-adjusted to 2025 dollars)", y = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank())
```
:::
:::::

- **\~72% break even overall** — but the 2000s dipped below half, then the 2010s-2020s recovered to 80-90%
- **Nintendo & Sega** lead (median \> \$400M); publishers vary widely

::: notes
:::

## What I tried first

::::: columns
::: {.column width="55%"}
```{r}
#| label: q1-alternative-plot
#| fig-width: 5.6
#| fig-height: 4.3
q1_profitability |>
  ggplot(aes(budget_midpoint, worldwide_box_office, color = release_era)) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray54") +
  geom_point(size = 2.2, alpha = 0.85) +
  scale_x_log10(labels = scales::label_dollar(scale_cut = scales::cut_short_scale())) +
  scale_y_log10(labels = scales::label_dollar(scale_cut = scales::cut_short_scale())) +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set1", name = "Era") +
  labs(x = "Budget (midpoint)", y = "Worldwide box office") +
  theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank())
```
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}
A single scatter colored by era.

- The overall trend is visible...
- ...but you **can't tell how each decade did** for break-even — the colors blend together.
- **Faceting** into one panel per decade fixed that.
:::
:::::

::: notes
:::

# Question 2: Game vs. film ratings

## Games always out-review their films

::::: columns
::: {.column width="52%"}
```{r}
#| label: dumbbell-ratings-plot
#| fig-alt: "A dumbbell chart of 28 franchises sorted by rating gap. Each row shows the average film score (red) vs. the average game score (blue). The gray line that connects both represents the width of the gap. Every franchise shows game scores (blue) averaging higher than their respective film scores, which means that overall games scored higher. While 'House of the Dead' and 'Dungeon Siege' seem to have the widest gaps at the top of the chart, 'Angry Birds' and 'Rampage' sit at the bottom with the narrowest gaps."
#| fig-width: 5.2
#| fig-height: 4.0
ggplot(q2_franchise_ratings, aes(y = fct_reorder(franchise, rating_gap))) +
  geom_segment(aes(x = film_score, xend = game_score,
                   yend = fct_reorder(franchise, rating_gap)),
               color = "gray72", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(data = q2_long, aes(x = score, color = type), size = 2.6) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Film" = "#E41A1C", "Game" = "#377EB8"), name = NULL) +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 100)) +
  labs(
    x = "Critic score (Metacritic)", y = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
    legend.position = "top"
  )
```
:::

::: {.column width="48%"}
```{r}
#| label: q2-rating-gap-by-era-plot
#| fig-alt: "A boxplot showing the game-to-film Metacritic rating gap (average game score minus average film score) across four eras defined by a franchise's first film: 1990s and earlier, 2000s, 2010s, and 2020s, with each franchise shown as a gray dot. Overall, boxes are above the zero line, so games have a higher rating than films in every era. The medians do not seem to trend in one direction (circa 36, 48, 31, and 44), and the later eras only have a few franchises to draw upon."
#| fig-width: 4.8
#| fig-height: 4.0
ggplot(q2_franchise_ratings, aes(release_era, rating_gap, fill = release_era)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.85, outlier.shape = NA, width = 0.6) +   # outliers are hidden
  geom_jitter(width = 0.12, size = 1.8, alpha = 0.6, color = "gray28") +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Blues") +
  labs(
    x = "Era of the franchise's first film", y = "Rating gap (Metacritic score)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
    legend.position = "none"
  )
```
:::
:::::

- **All 28 franchises**: games score higher than their films (avg gap **\~39 pts**)
- Across eras: **no clear trend** — games win consistently, whenever the franchise started

::: notes
:::

## What I tried first

::::: columns
::: {.column width="55%"}
```{r}
#| label: q2-alternative-plot
#| fig-width: 5.4
#| fig-height: 4.4
q2_franchise_ratings |>
  mutate(label = if_else(rating_gap > 50 | rating_gap < 20, franchise, NA_character_)) |>
  ggplot(aes(game_score, film_score)) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray52") +
  geom_point(size = 2.4, alpha = 0.9, color = "#377EB8") +
  geom_text(aes(label = label), na.rm = TRUE, size = 2.6, vjust = -0.7, color = "gray30") +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 100)) + scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 100)) +
  coord_equal() +
  labs(x = "Game score", y = "Film score") +
  theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank())
```
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}
A scatter with a parity line.

- Every point fell **below the line**, so they all bunched together...
- ...and the **labels overlapped**.
- The **dumbbell** gave each franchise its own row and made the gap the line's length.
:::
:::::

::: notes
:::

# Thank you!

## Takeaways

- **Commercially**, game-to-film adaptations are more profitable than ever — especially since 2010
- **Critically**, the games still win — *every* franchise's games out-scored its films

*Thank you for watching! Please let me know if you have any questions.*

::: notes
:::
